nativeRange.habitat: Adults are generally encountered in deep water adjacent to reef drop-offs, hovering above sand and rubble slopes typically between 30–80 m (recorded range 18–100 m). They construct rubble burrows from shell fragments and coral debris and retreat into them when alarmed.

decorPreferences: deep sandy substrate at least 7–10 cm (3 in) for burrow construction
scattered coral rubble and shell fragments for burrow material
open swimming area with unobstructed mid-water space
tight-fitting lid or canopy with no open gaps (strong jumper)
well-established mature aquarium with stable parameters
rockwork with caves and crevices around the tank periphery

diet.notes: In the wild, generally feeds on zooplankton — copepods, small planktonic crustaceans, pelagic tunicates, and drifting eggs picked from the water column above the substrate. In captivity, it has been reported to eventually accept a varied meaty diet of frozen mysis, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped marine fish and shrimp flesh, and small zooplankton-type frozen foods, with multiple small feedings per day generally advised. Newly imported specimens often refuse prepared foods initially and may need live mysis, live brine, or glass/black worms to begin feeding; starvation in the first weeks after import is a commonly cited cause of loss.

goodWith: other small peaceful reef fish (small wrasses, firefish, flasher wrasses)
ornamental shrimp and other reef inverts
avoidWith: boisterous or aggressive tankmates that monopolise feeding (large dottybacks, triggers, aggressive damsels)
fast-feeding tankmates that outcompete a timid planktivore
very brightly-lit shallow-reef setups without shaded refuges
open-top tanks without a tight-fitting lid
conspecifics except as an established male/female pair

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Jokuyken15; 4664x3109. Sharp, vibrant live in-water individual clearly showing the diagnostic white body and bold red stripe running from snout through the eye to the dark caudal fin.

summary: The Skunk Tilefish (Hoplolatilus marcosi) is a small Indo-Pacific reef fish reaching around 12 cm, recognized by its white body and a bold red stripe running from the snout to the tail. It is generally regarded as an advanced-care species: peaceful and reef-safe, but a strong jumper and often difficult to feed on arrival.
careNotes:
**Identity and trade names.** The Skunk Tilefish is also sold as Redback Sand Tilefish, Red-stripe Tilefish, Redstripe(d) Tilefish, and Marcosi Tilefish. It is a white-bodied fish with a sharp red stripe running from the snout through the eye to the caudal fin, sometimes overlaid with subtle blue tints on the fins.

**Origin and depth.** Native to the Indo-Pacific, centred on the Coral Triangle (Indonesia, Philippines, Palau, Papua New Guinea, Solomon Islands), with records from Japan/Okinawa, East Timor, and Tonga. In the wild it is generally encountered at comparatively deep water (30–80 m, recorded down to 100 m) along sand-and-rubble slopes adjacent to reef drop-offs. Decompression-related injuries from too-fast collection ascents are a frequently cited cause of poor long-term survival in captivity, so well-handled individuals from a reputable supplier are strongly preferred.

**Tank and substrate.** The Skunk Tilefish needs a mature, stable reef aquarium with a deep soft sandy substrate — at least 7–10 cm (roughly 3 in) — that it can excavate into a burrow, along with scattered coral rubble and shell fragments it will use to reinforce the entrance. An open mid-water swimming lane is generally considered important, as the species hovers above the sand and picks food from the water column rather than grazing the rockwork. Subdued lighting and stable water parameters are commonly recommended.

**Jumping risk.** The Skunk Tilefish is widely regarded as a strong jumper that will leave the tank when startled. A tight-fitting lid or fully enclosed canopy with no open gaps is generally considered essential; this is one of the most consistently reported husbandry requirements for the species.

**Pairing.** Wild adults form obligate monogamous pairs — one-to-one bonded pairs that sometimes loosely associate with other pairs in small colonies. Pair-housing is generally considered the most successful long-term approach in captivity; pair-keeping is commonly described as helping newly introduced fish settle more quickly. Multiple unpaired conspecifics in the same tank are generally discouraged.

**Feeding difficulty (key concern).** The Skunk Tilefish is widely regarded as difficult to establish on prepared foods — newly imported specimens may refuse anything for days to weeks and have been reported to starve before they begin eating. Live mysis, live enriched brine shrimp, glass worms, and black worms are commonly recommended to coax initial feeding; frozen mysis, frozen brine, finely chopped seafood, and other prepared meaty foods are generally accepted once the fish is settled. Multiple small feedings per day are generally advised, partly because the species tends to be timid and is easily out-competed at feeding time by more assertive tankmates.

**Reef safety.** The Skunk Tilefish is generally considered reef-safe. Its wild diet is zooplankton, so it tends to ignore corals, clams, ornamental shrimp, and other reef invertebrates.
breedingNotes: Forms obligate monogamous one-to-one pairs in the wild, with both fish sharing burrow construction and maintenance. Captive spawning is rarely reported and successful larval rearing is not documented in the home-aquarium literature; commercial captive-bred specimens are not generally available.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary retailer listing for the Skunk Tilefish (Hoplolatilus marcosi). Source for adult size (5 in), 50 gal minimum tank, peaceful temperament, reef-compatible, 'Difficult' care level, carnivore diet with frozen mysis/brine/chopped shrimp, water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, SG 1.020–1.025), pair-preference behaviour, 3-in sandy-substrate requirement for burrowing, and the strong-jumper / tight-lid warning. Originally referenced via the collection page (collections/dartfish).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Authoritative source for taxonomy (Malacanthidae / Eupercaria), max size 12.0 cm TL and 10.4 cm SL common, depth range 18–100 m typical 30–80 m, temperature 22–28°C, Indo-Pacific distribution (Indonesia, Palau, Philippines, PNG, Solomon Islands, Tonga), zooplankton diet, reef-associated habitat with rubble burrow construction, obligate monogamous pair behaviour, and IUCN Least Concern status.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (5 in), minimum tank 55 gal, peaceful temperament, reef compatibility, moderate care level, carnivore diet of amphipods/copepods/zooplankton in the wild plus frozen/live meaty foods in captivity, water parameters (72–80°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), jumping risk requiring a sealed hood, and the 3-in minimum sand-bed requirement for burrowing.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Trade wholesaler care sheet. Cross-checked adult size (just over 5 in), 50 gal minimum tank, peaceful/timid temperament, reef-safe, dimly-lit aquarium preference, feeding behaviour — fish 'will readily accept most prepared meaty diets' once acclimated but live enriched brine, glass worms or black worms are commonly needed during the initial adjustment period — and the tight-lid jumping warning. Source for the 'Redstripe / Redback Tilefish' alternative trade names.

additional.name: Monaco Nature Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Naturalist encyclopedia entry. Cross-checked taxonomy (Perciformes / Malacanthidae), 12 cm max size, 18–80 m depth range, full Indo-Pacific range, obligate monogamous pair behaviour, elaborate rubble-mound burrow construction, and zooplankton feeding (copepods, pelagic tunicates, drifting eggs). Important husbandry note: imported specimens commonly have livers compromised by cyanide-style collection methods and swim bladders damaged by too-fast ascents — captive survival is often poor for that reason.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Hobby care-sheet site. Cross-checked 12 cm adult size, peaceful temperament, reef-safe status, recommended pair-housing, monogamous pair-bond behaviour, tight-fitting lid requirement, varied meaty diet (mysis, brine, finely chopped seafood), and a narrower water-parameter range (24–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.022–1.025). Notes a lower minimum tank figure (150 L / 40 gal) at the bottom of the recorded source spread.

fish.breedingNotes: Obligate monogamous pair-forming in the wild. Captive spawning is rarely reported and larval rearing has not been documented in the home-aquarium literature; commercial captive-bred stock is not generally available.
